_meta.notes: Chapter Review for Chapter 1. Optional. 1-2 connection teach cards + quiz covering all chapter material. Full vocab: kumusta, mabuti, ikaw, magandang, umaga, gabi, po, naman, sige, oo, hindi, opo, salamat, walang anuman, pasensya na, siyempre, pare, mare, ako, ka, siya, tayo, kami, kayo, sila.

cards.body: Everything you've learned has two modes: casual (with friends) and respectful (with elders and authority). The same words, different endings.

cards.prompt: It's morning. Greet your boss respectfully and ask how they are.
cards.explanation: Magandang umaga po! Kumusta po kayo? Full respectful morning greeting.

cards.prompt: Reply respectfully, naturally, and ask her back.
options: Mabuti naman po, kumusta po kayo?
cards.explanation: Mabuti naman po (I'm good, respectfully) + kumusta po kayo? (how are you, formal). Full respectful exchange.

cards.source_text: Match the casual word with its respectful version.
cards.explanation: Oo (casual yes) → Opo (respectful yes). Oo + po = opo.

cards.source_text: Your friend Jun asks: Are you free Saturday?
cards.prompt: Obviously yes. What do you say?
cards.explanation: Siyempre, pare! = Of course, bro! Casual and enthusiastic with a male friend.

cards.body: Notice how every greeting changes meaning with a different pronoun. Kumusta ka? Kumusta siya? Kumusta kayo? Same structure, different people.

cards.source_text: You and Maria went to a party. Your coworker (who wasn't there) asks how it was.
cards.explanation: Kami = we (excluding the listener). Your coworker wasn't at the party.

cards.source_text: Someone gives you directions to the CR.
You arrive late to dinner with your boss.
cards.explanation: Pasensya na po = sorry (respectful). Apologize first when you're late.
Naman is a softener. It makes things sound friendlier and more natural.

cards.prompt: It's evening. You run into your friend Maria. Greet her casually and ask how she is.
cards.explanation: Magandang gabi! Kumusta ka? Casual evening greeting with a friend.

cards.source_text: Someone says walang anuman to you.
cards.prompt: What did you probably say right before?
cards.explanation: Walang anuman = you're welcome. They're responding to your salamat (thank you).

cards.prompt: Your grandmother offers you food. Say yes respectfully and thank her.
cards.explanation: Opo, salamat po! Yes (respectful) + thank you (respectful). Perfect for elders.

wrong_answer_tracking.behaviour: Wrong answers go to back of current session queue. User must get ALL questions right to complete. Cannot finish lesson with outstanding wrong answers.
wrong_answer_tracking.wrong_count_usage: Feed to Gemini system prompt for AI Practice.

alam_mo_ba: You just learned 25 Tagalog words. That's enough to greet anyone, thank anyone, apologize to anyone, and talk about anyone. Not bad.
The respect system (po, opo, kayo) you learned is uniquely Filipino. No other language in Southeast Asia handles respect in quite the same way.
Everything in Chapter 1 works in real life right now. Try saying 'Kumusta po?' to a Filipino family member today.
The Spanish influence on Tagalog (kumusta, sige, siyempre, pare, mare, pasensya) comes from 333 years of colonisation (1565-1898). You'll keep noticing it.
